title: Cloudy Urine in Cats: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Noticed cloudy urine on your floor? Learn what causes cat urinary issues, why it is a medium-level urgency, and how photos help your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: Cloudy cat urine typically indicates an infection, crystals, or inflammation and requires a veterinary visit within 24–48 hours; however, seek emergency care immediately if your cat is straining or unable to pass urine.

What does it mean if my cat's urine is cloudy?

  • Normally, a cat's urine should be clear and pale yellow.
  • Cloudiness indicates the presence of material that should not be there, such as crystals, bacteria, white blood cells (pus), red blood cells (blood), or excess protein.
  • It is a sign that your cat's urinary tract is currently inflamed or under stress.

When is cloudy urine in cats considered a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for cloudy urine is generally Medium, as it usually indicates discomfort rather than an immediate threat.
  • If your cat is also unable to pass any urine, visiting the litter box frequently, straining, or crying out, this indicates a life-threatening blockage and is a critical emergency.
  • If they are still successfully producing urine, you should schedule a veterinary appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours.

How does taking a photo of the urine help my veterinarian?

  • Urine is quickly absorbed into litter, so finding a sample on a hard floor provides a unique diagnostic opportunity.
  • A clear photo allows your vet to see the exact color and degree of opacity before the sample is cleaned.
  • Visual evidence of flecks of blood or mucus helps the veterinary team prioritize your appointment.
  • The photo can help your vet differentiate between a simple infection and more complex crystal formation.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Cloudy urine in cats warrants investigation via urinalysis, including assessment of color and turbidity. Microscopic examination of urine sediment should be performed to evaluate for red blood cells, white blood cells, epithelial cells, bacteria, yeast, and crystals. Since delay in analyzing urine samples can result in artifacts, prompt analysis or refrigeration is important. Urolithiasis, a common cause of lower urinary signs, is often suspected based on hematuria, dysuria, or urethral obstruction, and urinalysis, urine culture, radiography, and ultrasonography may be required for diagnosis. Radiography, cystoscopy, or ultrasonography are critically important to detect uroliths, as abdominal palpation is unreliable; double-contrast radiography may be required for smaller uroliths.

Chapter: Urology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1531)

faqs.question: Can cloudy urine wait until the weekend is over?
faqs.answer: If your cat is eating, drinking, and successfully passing urine, it can usually wait 24 hours. However, if your cat is a male and is straining without producing urine, you must go to an emergency clinic immediately.

faqs.question: Should I try to collect a sample of the urine?
faqs.answer: Yes, if possible. You can use a clean syringe or a sterile container to pick up the liquid from the floor. Keep it refrigerated and bring it to your appointment.

faqs.question: What are common causes of cloudiness?
faqs.answer: The most common causes include Urinary Tract Infections (UTIs), Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD), and the presence of bladder stones or crystals.
